title: Screens, Cassettes, Film Composition, and the Heel Effect
narrationScript: Most of the radiation that reaches an industrial X-ray film flies straight through it without leaving a mark. The emulsion is thin and the photons are energetic, so the film by itself captures only about one percent of what arrives. Intensifying screens exist to claw back some of that wasted energy, and the standard tool of film radiography is the lead screen. A lead screen is a thin foil of lead, usually bonded to a card for stiffness, pressed against each side of the film. The front screen, the one facing the source, is typically five thousandths of an inch thick. The back screen is typically ten thousandths. When high energy photons strike the lead they knock loose photoelectrons, and those electrons expose the emulsion at point blank range. The film cannot efficiently stop photons, but it stops electrons very well. Above roughly one hundred twenty five kilovolts the lead gives a net gain, commonly cutting exposure by a factor of two to four. Below that energy the foil absorbs more primary beam than its electrons give back, which is why lead screens and low kilovoltage work do not mix. The lead earns its keep twice, because it also filters. Scattered radiation arrives at lower energy and from odd angles, so the foil absorbs it preferentially while passing the image forming beam. The screens speed up the exposure and clean up the image at the same time. The front screen is kept thinner so it does not eat the primary beam on the way in, and the thicker back screen mops up backscatter coming from behind the cassette. Fluorescent screens work on a different principle. Instead of emitting electrons, a phosphor layer glows with visible and ultraviolet light when radiation strikes it, and the light exposes the film. The payoff is speed. Intensifying factors of ten to one hundred or more are possible, which can turn an hour long exposure into a minute. The price is definition. Light spreads sideways in a way electrons do not. Every glowing crystal acts like a tiny floodlight, so the image goes soft and picks up a mottled, grainy texture. That is why pure fluorescent screens are generally not permitted where code level sensitivity is required. Fluorometallic screens, lead foil combined with a phosphor layer, split the difference and are accepted by some specifications with particular films. The number that describes all of this is the intensifying factor. Divide the exposure needed without screens by the exposure needed with them for the same film density. If a shot needs twelve units bare and four units with screens, the factor is three. Whatever screen you use, it must touch the film everywhere. Electrons and light spread out across any gap, and a region of poor contact prints as a locally fuzzy patch that can hide a fine crack while the rest of the film looks perfect. Rigid cassettes use springs or felt pads to clamp the sandwich flat. Flexible vinyl cassettes rely on strapping and the curvature of the part. Vacuum cassettes pull the stack into intimate contact and give the best result on critical work. Beyond holding the sandwich, a cassette has two more jobs. It must be absolutely light tight, and its front face must absorb as little radiation as possible. Screens also have to be clean to do honest work. Dirt, lint, grease, or a flake of dried chemistry between screen and film blocks the electrons locally and prints a light mark on the radiograph that can mimic or mask an indication. Scratches and dents print as well. So handle screens by the edges, store cassettes flat and loaded, and inspect screens under a strong light regularly. Clean lead screens with mild detergent or a commercial screen cleaner on a lint free wipe, stroking along the length rather than in circles. Light oxidation can be removed gently with the finest grade of steel wool where the maker allows it. Dry everything thoroughly, and retire any screen with deep scratches, creases, or corrosion. The film itself is a precise layered product. At the center is a polyester base about seven thousandths of an inch thick, tinted faint blue, tough and dimensionally stable so that measurements taken on the film mean something. Thin adhesive layers bond an emulsion of silver halide crystals in gelatin to each side, and a hardened gelatin supercoat protects each face from abrasion. Industrial film carries emulsion on both sides, which roughly doubles its speed and the density it can reach. One last behavior to know about X-ray tubes. The beam is not equally intense across its own width. Photons emerging at shallow angles toward the anode end of the field pass through more target metal on their way out and are partly absorbed, so the anode side of the field runs measurably weaker than the cathode side. This is the heel effect, and it grows with steeper target angles, shorter distances, and wider fields. The practical move is simple. When one end of the part is thicker, point the cathode end of the field at the thick section, and keep precision work inside the uniform middle of the beam.
imagePrompt: Under dim red safelight in a darkroom, a technician in nitrile gloves slides a sheet of industrial X-ray film between two thin lead intensifying screens inside an open flexible cassette on a spotless stainless steel workbench.

sections.heading: Why Screens: Lead Intensification
sections.body:
Industrial X-ray film by itself is an inefficient detector: the emulsion is thin and radiographic photons are energetic, so the film absorbs only about **1%** of the radiation reaching it. **Intensifying screens** recover part of the loss. The standard in film radiography is the **lead screen**: thin lead foil, usually bonded to a stiffening card, pressed against each side of the film - typically **0.005 in. on the front** (source side) and **0.010 in. on the back**.

Lead screens intensify by **electron emission**. Incident photons knock photoelectrons out of the foil, and those electrons - which the emulsion stops far more readily than photons - expose the film at contact range. The effect becomes a net gain above roughly **125 kV**; below that, the foil absorbs more primary beam than its electrons return, so lead screens and low-kV work do not mix.

The foil earns its keep a second way: **scatter filtration**. Scattered radiation is lower in energy and arrives obliquely, so the lead absorbs it preferentially while passing the image-forming primary beam - the screens speed up the exposure and clean up the image at the same time. The front screen is the thinner of the pair so it does not attenuate the primary beam excessively on the way in; the thicker back screen also stops **backscatter** arriving from behind the cassette.

sections.caption: The exposure sandwich: front and back lead screens emit photoelectrons into the film while filtering scatter, and the pressure backing keeps everything in contact.

sections.heading: Fluorescent Screens and Intensifying Factors
sections.body:
**Fluorescent (salt) screens** intensify with light instead of electrons: a phosphor layer - calcium tungstate or rare-earth compounds - glows under radiation, and the light exposes the film. The payoff is speed: **intensifying factors from roughly 10 to 100 or more**, against about **2 to 4** for lead foil at typical radiographic energies. The price is definition. Every glowing crystal is a tiny floodlight; light diffuses sideways into the film, and the image picks up unsharpness and **screen mottle**. Pure fluorescent screens are therefore generally **not permitted for high-sensitivity code work**, though they earn their keep where speed matters more than sensitivity. **Fluorometallic screens** - lead foil plus phosphor - sit between the two, offering intermediate speed with better image quality than salt screens when paired with films designed for them.

The **intensifying factor (IF)** quantifies all of this:

- **IF = exposure required without screens ÷ exposure required with screens**, for the same film density

If a shot needs 12 mA·min bare and 4 mA·min with screens, IF = 3. Factors are not constants - they rise and fall with radiation energy, because lead's electron yield and a phosphor's light output each have their own response curves. That is one more reason an exposure chart belongs to a specific film-screen combination and cannot be borrowed across techniques.

sections.title: Contact is everything
sections.body: Electrons from lead foil and light from phosphors **spread sideways across any gap** between screen and film. A region of poor contact prints as a **locally fuzzy patch** - unsharpness that can hide a fine crack while the rest of the film looks perfect. Rigid cassettes clamp the sandwich with springs or felt pads; **vacuum cassettes** pull it into intimate contact and give the best result on critical work. A wavy, creased, or dented screen can never make proper contact - replace it.

sections.heading: Screen Care, Cleaning, and Cassettes
sections.body:
Anything between screen and film prints on the radiograph. **Dirt, lint, grease, or a flake of dried chemistry blocks the local electron intensification and leaves a light mark**; a scratch or gouge in the foil prints as a line; creases print as streaks. These artifacts can mimic indications - or mask real ones - so screen condition is an image-quality issue, not housekeeping.

Working practice:
- **Handle screens by the edges**; never slide film across a dusty screen
- **Inspect screens under a strong light** at every loading session; retire any screen with deep scratches, dents, creases, or corrosion
- **Clean lead screens** with mild detergent and water or a commercial screen cleaner on lint-free wipes, stroking along the screen's length, and dry thoroughly
- **Light oxidation** may be removed gently with the finest grade of steel wool where the manufacturer allows - never coarse abrasives that gouge the foil
- **Clean fluorescent screens** only with the phosphor manufacturer's recommended cleaner and soft lint-free materials

The **cassette** holds the sandwich. Rigid cassettes (low-absorption front, spring or felt pressure backing) suit flat work; **flexible vinyl cassettes** strap tight to curved parts; **vacuum cassettes** give the best contact of all. Every type must be **completely light-tight** - fogged film edges betray a leak - and the front face must be a **low-absorption material** that does not filter the image-forming beam.

sections.heading: Industrial Film, Layer by Layer
sections.body:
Industrial radiographic film is a layered precision product:
- **Base** - polyester about **0.007 in.** thick, tinted faint blue, tough and dimensionally stable so measurements taken on the film are meaningful
- **Subbing layers** - thin adhesive coats bonding emulsion to base
- **Emulsion** - microscopic **silver halide crystals dispersed in gelatin**, roughly 0.001 in. per side
- **Supercoat** - a hardened gelatin skin protecting each emulsion from abrasion during handling and processing

The emulsion is coated on **both sides** of the base. Double coating roughly doubles film speed and the density attainable for a given exposure, while keeping each emulsion thin enough to develop evenly.

When radiation - or a screen electron - strikes a silver halide grain, it frees electrons that reduce a few silver ions to metallic silver at sensitivity sites. That trace is the **latent image**: invisible, but chemically primed. The developer later amplifies it grain by grain into visible blackening; the fixer dissolves away the unexposed grains. Grain size sets the classic trade - **finer grain means slower film but a sharper, lower-noise image** - which is the basis of the film system classes covered with processing later in the course.

sections.caption: Industrial film in cross-section: a blue-tinted polyester base carrying subbing, silver-halide emulsion, and protective supercoat on both sides.

sections.heading: The Heel Effect
sections.body:
An X-ray beam is not uniformly intense across its own field. X-rays are generated slightly **inside** the angled anode, so photons emerging at shallow angles - toward the **anode end** of the field - must escape through more target metal and are partially absorbed in the target's 'heel.' The result: **intensity falls off toward the anode side** of the field, while the cathode side runs noticeably stronger.

The heel effect is more pronounced with:
- **Steeper (smaller) anode target angles**
- **Shorter source-to-film distances**
- **Larger film formats and wider fields**

Field practice:
- **Orient the part with its thicker section toward the cathode end** of the field, putting the stronger side of the beam over the greater absorber and evening out film density
- **Keep precision work within the central, uniform portion** of the beam when density balance matters
- When a uniform-thickness part shows a smooth density slope from one end of the film to the other, suspect the heel effect before blaming processing

Gamma sources emit essentially uniformly in all directions - the heel effect is purely an X-ray tube phenomenon.

sections.caption: Self-absorption in the angled anode weakens the beam toward the anode end of the field - so the thicker part section goes toward the cathode.

figures.title: Inside the cassette: the exposure sandwich

checkQuestions.question: Why is the front lead screen typically 0.005 in. while the back screen is 0.010 in.?
options: The front screen must pass the image-forming primary beam with minimal loss, while the thicker back screen also stops backscatter
The front screen wears out faster, so it starts thinner
Thicker front screens would make the image too dark
The back screen needs extra weight to hold the film flat
checkQuestions.explanation: The front foil sits in the primary beam path, so it is kept thin to intensify without excessive attenuation; the back foil can be thicker because it works after the film and doubles as a backscatter barrier.

checkQuestions.question: A weld shot requires 12 mA·min with bare film but only 4 mA·min with screens for the same density. The intensifying factor is:
checkQuestions.explanation: IF = exposure without screens ÷ exposure with screens = 12 ÷ 4 = 3, a typical value for lead foil at radiographic energies.

checkQuestions.question: Fluorescent screens can cut exposure times dramatically, yet they are generally not used for high-sensitivity code radiography because:
options: Their light diffuses sideways, producing unsharpness and screen mottle that degrade definition
They cannot be cleaned once contaminated
They require a vacuum cassette to function
checkQuestions.explanation: Phosphor light spreads laterally into the film in a way contact-range electrons do not, so the speed gain comes at the cost of image definition - unacceptable where code sensitivity is required.

checkQuestions.question: A small tuft of lint was trapped between the front lead screen and the film during exposure. On the radiograph it will most likely appear as:
options: A dark spot, because lint fluoresces
A light mark, because the lint locally blocked the electron intensification
Nothing - lint is transparent to radiation
A fogged band across the whole film
checkQuestions.explanation: Foreign material between screen and film stops the photoelectrons that would have boosted local exposure, so the area receives less total exposure and prints lighter - an artifact that can mask or mimic an indication.

checkQuestions.question: A long, uniform-thickness plate is radiographed in one X-ray exposure, and the film shows a smooth density decrease toward one end with no thickness change to explain it. The likely cause and fix are:
options: Backscatter; add lead behind the cassette
The heel effect; the weak end of the field was the anode side - orient thicker sections toward the cathode and work within the uniform center of the beam
The inverse square law; move the film closer
checkQuestions.explanation: Self-absorption in the anode heel weakens the anode end of the field, producing a density slope across a uniform part. Orienting the cathode end toward thicker material - or staying in the central beam - balances the exposure.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiography Technique 2.2-2.5
Manufacturer guidance for radiographic screen care and cleaning (general practice)
General references: industrial film construction, intensifying screens, anode heel effect
